coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained a lot of attention and have been investigated extensively over the last decade due to their exceptional physical and chemical qualities including water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, tiny size, fluorescence, and simplicity of modification. The employment of more ecologically friendly technologies to prepare high-quality CDs remains a challenge. The use of renewable, low-cost, and green biomass resources is not only required for large-scale synthesis of natural product CDs but also supports the development of sustainable applications, particularly in pesticide detection. Pesticide detection is critical because pesticides are commonly used in agriculture and the environment, potentially harming human health and the ecosystem. In this review, we discuss the most recent breakthroughs in natural product CDs, such as the detection of pesticides from various carbon sources using the applied sensing method. High sensitivity and selectivity pesticide sensors are critically needed to control food safety, protect ecosystems, and prevent disease. Therefore, we also present an overview of current breakthroughs and new trends in optical sensors for pesticide detection based on fluorescence and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) paired with pesticide-specific aptamer recognition element types based on CDs.

head.ce:title: Recent advanced carbon dots derived natural products and aptasensor-based carbon dots for detection of pesticides

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have gained a lot of attention and have been investigated extensively over the last decade due to their exceptional physical and chemical qualities including water solubility, low toxicity, biocompatibility, tiny size, fluorescence, and simplicity of modification. The employment of more ecologically friendly technologies to prepare high-quality CDs remains a challenge. The use of renewable, low-cost, and green biomass resources is not only required for large-scale synthesis of natural product CDs but also supports the development of sustainable applications, particularly in pesticide detection. Pesticide detection is critical because pesticides are commonly used in agriculture and the environment, potentially harming human health and the ecosystem. In this review, we discuss the most recent breakthroughs in natural product CDs, such as the detection of pesticides from various carbon sources using the applied sensing method. High sensitivity and selectivity pesticide sensors are critically needed to control food safety, protect ecosystems, and prevent disease. Therefore, we also present an overview of current breakthroughs and new trends in optical sensors for pesticide detection based on fluorescence and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) paired with pesticide-specific aptamer recognition element types based on CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides (herbicides, insecticides, fungicides, and so on) have proven to have high commercial levels and are widely utilized to improve agricultural products in worldwide. The presence of pesticides has played a significant role in dealing with crop damage, which can lead to a drop in crop output quality [1]. Furthermore, pesticide use has helped to secure roughly a third of global crop production [2] and is responsible for growing agricultural product production [3]. According to FAO 2020 data, from 1990 to 2018, the average across the globe dose of pesticide use increased by 69.7%, more than doubling in major agricultural-producing regions such as Asia and America. [4]. As a result, pesticide residues will continue to rise. Pesticide residues that accumulate as a result of excessive use have major long-term consequences for ecosystems and human health and have hence grown into a worldwide issue today. Pesticide residues that are not degraded after a given period persists on the crop [5], soil [6], and water [7], posing health risks such as body metabolism, birth defects, reproductive system difficulties, respiratory disorders in people, and other diseases [8,9]. Therefore, effective pesticide contamination screening in agricultural products and periodic monitoring of pesticide spread in the environment are required to reduce the presence of pesticide residues on agricultural products before they are distributed to consumers.

Although many traditional methods have been used to detect pesticide residues in food or agricultural products, such as gas chromatography (GC), high-performance liquid chromatography (HLPC), mass spectroscopy (MS), and electrochemiluminescence (ECL), as well as voltammetric methods, these methods have high sensitivity and selectivity to detect the presence of pesticide residues. However, these technologies have numerous drawbacks, including high prices and equipment, the need for expertise, sophisticated operations, lengthy analysis times, the inability to obtain on-site analysis fast, and a laborious sample treatment preparation process [10,11]. These flaws continue to limit pesticide monitoring in real-time and promptly, making these technologies unsuitable for pesticide detection, particularly in distant locations with limited access. As a result, it is vital to design a simple optical sensing technology that can also be used by ordinary people.

In this context, simple optical sensing technology has received a great deal of interest for the detection of pesticide residues; basic equipment has high sensitivity and selectivity, and detection may be done directly on the spot, with rapid and accurate findings that are inexpensive and ecologically benign [12]. Several studies on fluorescent (FL) carbon dots (CDs) such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbon polymers dots (PDs) have been conducted in the last decade, and they serve as emerging alternatives in optical sensing technologies due to their unique chemical structure and optical properties. As shown in Fig. 1
, the classification of CDs is based on their carbon structure, surface functional groups, and their properties.

The GQDs are anisotropic graphene fragments that consist of monolayer or multiple layers of graphene sheets with graphene networks in their configuration. Due to the presence of diverse chemical functionalities on their edge or within their interlayer defect, GQDs exhibit quantum confinement and edge effects [13,14]. CQDs are crystalline nanospheres with a large number of chemical groups that give CDs' intrinsic state luminescence and quantum confinement effect [15,16]. The CNDs have a high degree of carbonization with edge effects, but the crystalline or polymeric structures are not revealed. Furthermore, CNDs do not exhibit the quantum confinement effect [17–19]. CPDs are perfectly crosslinked nanohybrids of carbon and aggregated polymers, with a central carbonized core surrounded by polymeric chains or functional groups [19,20].

The primary focus of this analysis is on CQD and/or CNDs, the largest group of CDs. Furthermore, due to the similarities of the spherical carbon structure, many publications still refer to carbon dots as carbon quantum dots or carbon nanodots [21]. Therefore, only carbon dots (CDs) will be mentioned further in this review. Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel type of zero-dimensional (0D) carbon nanomaterial that is spherical in size less than 10 nm and can emit a fluorescent [22]. This material was discovered in 2004 by Xu et al. during the purification process on single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) [23]. Subsequently, Sun et al. in 2006 verified the luminous properties of CDs using surface passivation and laser ablation. Since then, research on the nature of CDs has begun to advance considerably [24]. Subsequently, “carbon dots” (sometimes also called carbon quantum dots) became a comprehensive term for all types of fluorescent and surface-functionalized carbon nanoparticles. All CDs exhibit intrinsic luminescence and are frequently formed via sp2 or sp3 hybridization of carbon atoms with nearby carbon atoms, oxygen atoms, or other heteroatoms [25]. CDs can also be doped with other atoms (for example, oxygen or nitrogen), modified surface groups, and polymer aggregates. In brief, all CDs are formed up of an inner core of carbon that is surrounded by numerous surface modifications (passivation). These surface alterations not only improve colloid solubility and stability but also allow for additional functionalization with other species for specialized purposes, such as sensing systems or sensors.

CDs offer many fascinating properties, including great photostability, excellent water solubility, fluorescence, and outstanding biocompatibility, as well as being non-toxic and environmentally benign [26,27]. Furthermore, CDs exhibit optical features similar to typical semiconductor quantum dots, such as controllable conductivity, electron transfer, and light emission. Hence, they are a possible replacement for traditional perovskite quantum dots by overcoming their flaws of high toxicity and low biocompatibility. These distinctive properties have sparked substantial research in sensing[28,29], imaging [30], electrochemistry [31,32], and other fields. Simultaneously, there are several difficulties in the development of CDs, such as poor quantum yield (QY) and product yield. The mechanism of fluorescence is still a source of contention among scientists [33]. Through doping heteroatoms and passivating the surface, the researchers aim to improve the performance of CDs and increase their applications.

Significantly work has been made in recent years in synthesizing CDs, defining their characteristics, and researching their prospective applications. However, carbon sources and their significance have been largely ignored [34]. There have been numerous reports of CDs being produced using macromolecules (e.g., biomass, plant fiber, graphene, carbon nanotubes, etc.) and micro molecules (e.g., urea, ammonia, phenylenediamine, etc.). Due to the low yield of CDs, the cost of the source would be an essential consideration. Based on the advantages of availability, cheap cost, and renewability, biomass and its derivatives have received a lot of attention among all types of carbon sources. Natural product such as fruits [35,36], vegetables [37,38], flowers [39,40], and leaves [41,42] are used as initial carbon sources to prepare CDs. The extensive use of biomass can not only lower raw material costs but also achieve efficient carbon recycling. Furthermore, biomass-derived CDs exhibit outstanding biocompatibility and persistent fluorescence emission, making them useful in sensing, imaging, and other disciplines [43,44].

CDs can be synthesized through two approaches, “top–down” (laser ablation, chemical oxidation, etc.) and “bottom–up” (hydrothermal, microwave, electrochemistry, etc.) [45]. Based on the approach method used in CD synthesis, the hydrothermal approach method is commonly used because of some of the advantages it has, such as being inexpensive, environmentally friendly, easy to control, and very effective for the synthesis of CDs made from natural materials [34,46]. Furthermore, in this review, we will review the carbon sources used in the synthesis of CDs derived from natural materials as pesticide detection [37,47] [48]. The selection of carbon sources derived from natural materials in the synthesis of CDs has numerous advantages, including being inexpensive, easy to produce, environmentally friendly, non-toxic, and abundant in nature [49]. Furthermore, carbon sources derived from natural products are commonly used because they are rich in numerous elements such as C, O, N, S, and H, and they also contain heteroatoms, which are the best precursors for the synthesis of CDs [38]. Other factors to increase fluorescence in CDs, such as heteroatomic doping [50–52], must be considered in addition to the carbon source used in the synthesis of CDs for pesticide detection. Finally, the detection sensitivity and selectivity factor in a pesticide is also a major challenge when synthesizing an optical sensing technology, such as by modifying CDs with recognition elements such as aptamer [53].

Several reviews focus on the synthesis of CNDs from natural and synthetic materials and their applications [34,49,54] [55]. However no published work has yet focused on the concurrent discussion of CNDs derived from natural materials and modifications of CNDs mediated by aptamers as pesticide detection. Therefore, this work focuses on literature containing the most recent research on the synthesis of CNDs derived from natural materials with aptamer modifications for pesticide detection. Furthermore, this review is the first to reveal the pH values of CNDs solutions, where the pH value affects the intensity of CNDs fluorescence for pesticide detection [56].

A systematic literature review (SLR) often referred to as a systematic review is a form of secondary study to identify, evaluate, and interpret all research relevant to predetermined research questions [57]. Literature searches are limited to articles published in 2018 to 2023. The titles and keywords used are “aptasensors for detection pesticides” AND “carbon dots OR carbon nanodots for pesticides” and “biomass OR natural product” AND “sensor pesticides” to search for publications in several databases such as Science direct, Pub Med, and MDPI.

Search for publications on each database using the “Advanced Research” option to narrow the search results to specific keywords. Furthermore, this SLR employs the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) analysis technique. There were 8012 papers identified in the initial search results, 4527 articles in Science direct, 3414 articles in Pub Med, and 76 articles in MDPI. Then, following screening based on the years 2018–2023, 4556 articles were obtained from the three databases, dropping 3461 articles not included in the literature study (excluding). Furthermore, filtering was done based on the type of article acquired, which included 3818 research articles, 490 review articles, and 248 other publications. Following a study of the title and abstract, 106 research publications were chosen for further review; however, only 109 were accessible, with 16 articles including duplicates and 72 articles being irrelevant to the topic, namely fluorometric aptasensors based carbon dots. As a result, the ultimate result is 21 research articles that will be thoroughly analyzed in this study. The literature search is described in more detail in Fig. 2 .
The problem that is in focus is based on the population, intervention, comparison, results, and context (PICOC) method with the following research questions:
(P) - What natural carbon sources, or so-called green carbon, can be used as the major constituent of CNDs in pesticide detection applications?
(I) - Which CDs are most widely used to detect pesticides as biosensors in combination with aptamers, known as aptasensors?
(C) - Which aptasensors-based CNDs are best for pesticide detection?
(O) - What is the outcome of the sensitivity and selectivity of the combination of aptasensors CNDs in pesticide detection?
(C) - Investigation of CNDs natural products and aptasensors based on CNDs in pesticide detection on real samples such as fruits and vegetables.

Furthermore, the 21 articles reviewed using the PICOC method above discuss CNDs derived from biomass carbon sources or natural products as pesticide detection [37–42,47,48,58–63] and CNDs combined with pesticide-specific aptamers, which we will subsequently identify aptasensors, as pesticide detection [53,64–69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs synthesis from natural product for detection of residue pesticides

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, regarding the “top-down” approaches, CDs are obtained by cutting or breaking down larger carbon structures, such as graphite oxide, carbon rods, and other large-size carbon source materials into nano size fragments by using physical or chemical techniques. Although the particles display a uniform size distribution, the QY is usually low, and strong chemicals and tedious post-surface passivation are involved. Due to such harsh conditions, these top-down methods are relatively tedious, in comparison to the bottom-up approaches.

Laser ablation is a conventional method that involves eliminating a part of a precursor material from the surface using a high-intensity laser beam. It enables the application of a controlled amount of energy to a specific target area and is commonly used to synthesize various carbon nanomaterials such as CDs [71], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [72], and nanodiamonds [73]. However, laser ablation generates merely a heterogeneous combination of solid carbon materials without or with weak PL. Then, for more scalable and faithful production of various types of CDs, an improved liquid-phase laser ablation method based on carbon suspensions in different solvents, either by themselves or those containing organic molecules or polymers to directly passivate the carbon NPs, has gained considerable attention. Nguyen et al. discovered that by modifying the combined parameters of laser fluence, spot size, and irradiation period, the size distribution and PL property of CDs generated via liquid-phase femtosecond laser ablation were well regulated. The average size of CDs gradually decreased as the laser fluence and spot size were reduced or the irradiation period was increased, and the PL spectra were blue-shifted due to the complex surface functional groups linked to CDs [74].

Chemical oxidation (Long et al., 2020) is a simple method for producing CDs that depends on the corrosive action of strong acids and oxidants to strip large-size carbon sources from top to bottom, or on redox processes to chemically generate and passivate CDs from bottom to top [75]. For example, hydrogen peroxide and oxidizing acids can be used to aid in the carbonization of natural materials for the fabrication of CDs. Natural components are often combined with concentrated nitric acid or concentrated sulfuric acid for a few days in a corrosion-resistant container to produce CDs. Following that, the resultant CDs can be separated and purified. Ultrasonic, stirring, and low-temperature heating treatments are routinely used to accelerate the process. Since the top-down strategy, the material performance of CDs has been modified by varying the degree of oxidation flaws [76]. However, this approach lacks exfoliate CDs selectively, and the molecular base structure is easily damaged, affecting their optical qualities. The chemical synthesis from bottom to top based on redox reactions can therefore overcome the above drawbacks to increase CD performance. Although the chemical oxidation approach is convenient for fast large-scale CD synthesis without the need for complex equipment, it is limited by the use of expensive oxidants that pollute the environment and the low production capacity owing to intermittent output [77]. Yan et al. synthesized CDs from starch and chemically oxidized them for the purpose of detecting the chemotherapeutic medication imatinib via a novel approach based on quenched fluorescence. Chemical oxidation was used to easily change the surface characteristics and fluorescence emission of CDs. Some issues, however, persist. The primary reason for employing CDs created by chemical oxidation is that the CDs solution may enhance biological toxicity because remaining chemical oxidation agents can contaminate the as-prepared CDs, which is a major disadvantage of this procedure [78]. In addition, Suryawanshi et al. created green CDs by oxidizing dead neem leaves with H2SO4 - HNO3. The produced CDs featured a variety of functional groups, including as -COOH and epoxy groups, which favor non-radiative integration of electron-hole pairs in the intrinsic state. The surface of the produced CDs was modified using amines. Meanwhile, the intrinsic fluorescence could be tweaked. The -COOH and epoxy groups were replaced with -CONH2 and -C-NH2 groups, which suppressed the non-radiative track. As a result, the fluorescence intensity rose because the intrinsic state emission increased. This work offered novel insights into CDs from an investigative and mechanistic standpoint, and it provided strategies for preparing multicolored CDs through oxidation [79].

Electrochemical oxidation method involves a nonselective “top - down” chemical cutting process of a carbon material such as graphite, carbon nanotubes, or carbon fiber electrodes [80,81]. However, C-dots generated using the current electrochemical methods have mixed sizes and must be separated further using filtration or chromatography to obtain monodisperse C-dots [82]. Furthermore, as-prepared C-dots have low photoluminescence efficiency (always less than 10%) [17,80–83], limiting further research in this field. Furthermore, the size-controllable preparation of high-quality C-dots using typical electrochemical procedures is challenging and complicated [80,82]. Exploration of new carbon sources for the simple, inexpensive, and controllable synthesis of such C-dots is very desirable from the standpoint of material synthesis has been intensively explored for green synthesis of CDs [84,85]. The top-down [85] and bottom-up [84] routes are suitable for electrochemical synthesis of diverse CDs. The operation process is usually carried out under alkaline conditions to gain uniform size of CDs for realizing low background noise, minimum self-quenching, and high sensitivity by changing the electrode material, electrode spacing, voltage, and current density. However, it is noted that the contact area between the electrode material and the solution surface increases in the reaction, which will affect the quality of the prepared CDs. To address this issue, Xu et al. reported an end-face electrochemical peeling strategy for scalable graphene preparation, providing a new idea for stabilizing the surface current and promoting the yield of cutting materials, as well as other carbon materials like CDs [86].

On the other hand, a simple and rapid ultrasonic technology has not only been utilized to shatter big carbon materials by the effect of very high energy ultrasonic sound waves but it has also been found useful in creating CDs from small molecules. In this method, ultrasound waves with low and high pressures generate small vacuum bubbles and distribute them uniformly throughout the solution [87,88]. Consequently, it prevents aggregation, resulting in the strong hydro-dynamic shear force and rapid infringement of the liquid jets [87,89]. By employing the energy generated via ultrasonic technique, large-sized carbon-based nanomaterials like graphite, activated carbon, and CNTs can be dissected into nanosized CDs [89,90]. Wang et al., for example, produced N-doped CDs from ascorbic acid and ammonia [91]. Dang et al. created white fluorescence CDs (used as white LEDs) using oligomer polyamide resin as a carbon precursor and ethylenediamine as a passionate. The as-prepared CDs were well distributed, with minimal crystallinity and functional groups at the surface [92].

Bottom-up approaches are currently popular due to benefits such as potential practical applicability, the use of non-toxic precursor chemicals, cost-effectiveness, simple instrumentation and reliable control, and simple and convenient methodology [4,87,93].

The hydrothermal and solvothermal method is the most commonly used due to their simple application and easily tunable properties, it has been the main approach to obtain CDs [94,95]. Many researchers employ this method due to it is a low-cost, environmentally benign, and efficient way to synthesize CNDs from a variety of organic materials. Carbon sources, such as carbohydrates, were dissolved in water with or without the use of chemicals (acid or alkali) [96]. The prepared solutions were placed in an autoclave, heated, and held at a consistent heating rate at a specific temperature. The supernatant, which contains big particles, was separated by centrifugation or dialysis. Natural products like as starch, maltose, glucose, sucrose, xylose, amylopectin, saccharose, and cellulose have frequently been employed as carbon sources to construct effective organic product-based CNDs for specific purposes [97,98]. Furthermore, the method allows producing nanostructures with high uniformity in size and shape [99]. Zhang et al. produced hydrothermally carbon dots with amplified photoluminescence intensity using lignocellulosic biomass. The values of the reaction parameters were 220 °C for 5 h [100]. Sun and Liu, synthesized CDs from plant source (Selenicereus grandiflorus) using hydrothermal method. The reaction was carried out at a low temperature and short time, 90 °C for 2 h [101]. There are many possibilities for variation of the reaction factors through this method. Temperatures can vary in a large range, from 90 °C to above 200 °C. Another crucial aspect of optimizing the reaction is time. Short times, such as 2 h, are viable for CDs formation. However, exploratory investigations concentrating on understanding the mechanisms involved and theoretical elements of this essential synthesis method are encouraged in order to optimize the reaction conditions as broadly as feasible.

Pyrolysis is an irreversible thermolysis reaction characterized by the chemical disintegration of organic molecules in an inert atmosphere. Pyrolysis causes both physical and chemical changes in organic material, resulting in solid carbonaceous residue. Pyrolysis is often performed under controlled pressure and at temperatures exceeding 430 °C (800 °F). Some organic carbon raw material sources can be used to create natural product-based CDs [102]. Wei et al. created fluorescent CNDs for bioimaging applications by pyrolyzing and direct calcination of gynostemma. Under natural light, the CNDs solution was pale yellow, but it fluoresced strongly blue under 365 nm UV light. Their average size was roughly 2.5 nm. Depending on the excitation wavelengths, the emitted wavelength has shifted to the red. Furthermore, even at high ionic strength circumstances, these CDs displayed good fluorescence stability. Bioimaging experiments in zebrafish were possible because to CDs' biocompatibility and good fluorescence stability. CDs can enter zebrafish embryos by the chorion or the mouth [103].

Template route is one of the advanced methods for QDs synthesis, first validated by Gu et al. in 2017. He created HKUST-1, ZIF-8, and MIL-101 CDs with wavelengths of ∼1.35 nm, ∼1.9 nm, and ∼3.4 nm respectively. In this approach, a highly porous metal–organic framework (MOFs) template, rich in reticular pores of 1–10 nm is used for CDs synthesis. In template-assisted synthesis, a soluble template is dipped and filled with precursor and kept at a high temperature of around 200 °C. The temperature and incubation duration may vary with the precursor that allows for the settling of the solution. The next step is the removal of the template without damaging the CDs which was done by solvent washing [104]. The most significant advantage of template-assisted synthesis is that the pores are regular in shape and size, yet it is still underutilized for natural product precursors.

Nature provides a nearly infinite supply of natural sources, which inspires scientists to develop new materials with novel applications and less environmental effect. Recently, much attention has focused on synthesizing natural product-derived carbon dots because of several advantages of natural products. Natural products that are extensively distributed in nature and are easily obtainable as well as continuously available, inexpensive, and renewable can replace the commonly used chemicals to prepare CDs [124]. These CDs-based natural products do not require organic solvents but instead can be prepared in aqueous solutions resulting in increased water solubility. Furthermore, most natural products possess complex components, which endow the CDs made from these green resources with abundant and various surface functional groups for specific sensing applications. The advantages of natural product-based CDs is summarized in Fig. 4 .

For every potential nanomaterial, the elements involved in the preparation plays a vital role. CDs-based natural products have improved optical properties and compound idleness [125]. Various natural product goods used as eco-friendly precursors include plant biomass, animal products, food items, biowastes, and waste materials, all of which contain proteins, starch, and lipids and are perfectly green and biocompatible carbon assets. There are numerous intrinsic benefits to exploiting these low-value precursor materials, such as natural bioresources or repurposing agricultural and industrial wastes. Similarly, utilization of plant extracts consisting of various bioactive molecules such as basic constituents (alkaloids), acidic components (tanins, flavonoids, ascorbic acid, tartaric acid, polyphenols, citric acid) and neutral ingredients (carbohydrates) built potent CDs because the synthetic kinetics depend upon these phytoconstituents. The bioactive components present in the extract deliver several benefits such as determination of surface functional groups on the CDs, material reactivity, particle size, and fluorescence quantum yield. To illustrate, neem leaf (Azadirachta indica) have better supercapacitor properties than Ashoka (Saraca asoca) due to the presence of the bioactive components present in Azadirachta indica [126]. The phytoconstituents such as certain enzymes, proteins, polysaccharides, vitamins, and other biomolecules possess natural behavior of reduction and capping of non-biocompatible substances [127]. The phytoconstituents present in CDs provide adaptability to solvent and dopants, hence making them an attractive choice for developing successful CDs.

Unlike man-made CDs, which require the addition of external nitrogen (N) or sulphur (S) containing compounds [128], these natural products proved more lucrative as they contain heteroatoms such as nitrogen and sulphur, therefore, can be conveniently prepared as heteroatom doped CDs [129]. Since, these raw materials are not only rich in carbon sources, but also allow for various heteroatom doping (N, S, P), hence addressing environmental difficulties caused by the employment of expensive/toxic chemical precursors, complex post-treatment processes, waste disposal, and so on [130]. Besides, to avoid any kind of toxicity, surface passivation via citric acid or amino acids are utilized as post synthesis treatment. On the other hand, one can avoid using or producing hazardous materials during the synthesis process by adopting the twelve main principles of green chemistry. These principles are multifaceted and propose environmentally favorable techniques [131,132] (Fig. 5 ).

In addition to the role of the carbon source element, recently, researchers have actively explored the environmental-friendly low-temperature hydrothermal method for synthesizing high-quality CDs based on natural products with various morphology, size, shape and surface functionalities to facilitate their broad applications in various sensors, specifically detection pesticides. The hydrothermal method of CDs from natural products is shown in Fig. 6 .

This approach has advantages such as environmentally friendly, simple, low-cost, and generally makes it possible to obtain CDs of a clear size and shape. The product diversity of CDs is primarily due to the diversity of carbon sources and strategies in their synthesis [133]. When producing CDs, there are two important things that can be ideally controlled by particle size and surface state, namely photoluminescence (PL) behavior and quantum output (QY). In addition, the quality and optical properties of CDs are also influenced by several parameters such as temperature, doping type, pH, and carbon precursors [134,135]. The source of carbon derived from natural products with their quantum output (QY) can be seen in Fig. 7 .

Purification of the post-synthesis CD reaction mixture is an important step in the creation of carbon dots since it contains a plethora of side-products and unreacted precursors of different solubility and size. Indeed, the elimination of side-products with different sizes and dispersibility profiles than the CDs would imply that a multi-step purification process is often necessary. Extensive purification methods are used to produce CDs with homogeneous physical and optical features [136]. Poor purification can limit the usage of CDs in some applications. For example, Essner et al. revealed that insufficient purification processes might have a significant impact on CD sensing performance due to the presence of fluorescent contaminants [137].

Carbon dot purification is typically achieved using a combination of multiple processes. Large amounts of insoluble materials are often removed by centrifugation and/or filtration [138]. In the former, insoluble contaminants will be found at the bottom of the tube after centrifugation, allowing easy separation from the supernatant, which normally contains the CDs. Impurities greater than the pore size of the filter will be removed from the filtrate in the latter case. Dialysis is another purification process that is often performed over a 24–72-h period to remove any remaining tiny soluble contaminants [139]. These three approaches are applied evenly (but in varied sequence) in green CD synthesis with the purpose of limiting the presence of any contaminants.

The presence of pH determination is equally vital in the synthesis of CDs as pesticide detection. Changes in pH can alter both the chemical structure and the electronic structure of the CDs, allowing it to operate as a sensor. Variations in electronic structure can result in variations in wavelength or fluorescence intensity, as illustrated in Fig. 8 . Changes in the molecule's structure can alter the orientation or distance between two fluorophores that act as donor-acceptor pairs. In some circumstances, changing the pH, for example, induces four distinct responses (reduction, increase in emissions, and red and blue changes), as observed by various organizations [140]. If the atomic structure of the synthesized CD s is known, the relationship between changes in pH and changes in fluorescence emission will be determined. For this reason, it is logical to provide comprehensive information in research using CDs as pesticide detection.

The schematic of the change in energy level of CDs due to pH is illustrated in Fig. 9(a). Choudhury et al. argued that pH conditions could substantially modify the nature of energy levels, which could affect the type of electronic transitions involved in CDs. This impact could be caused by the deprotonation of the functional groups that contain oxygen and are present on the surface of the CDs. This results in the progressive formation of several chemically distinct CDs species, which may raise the pH of the solution [143]. Hu et al. synthesized Leek as a precursor of CDs for dichlorvos sensitive detection, the group reported that the pH of the solution had a high impact on the fluorescence intensity ratio of NS-CDs between the presence and absence of the enzymatic system, and the highest efficiency was achieved at pH 8.0. At the same time, from the optimization results, it is found that the ion strength from 0 to 200 mM has a negligible effect on the fluorescence quenching efficiency [38].

The Aggregation of CDs is one of the pH response mechanisms depicted in Fig. 9(b). According to Zheng et al., the color of CDs in daylight changed from bright yellow to dark yellow for low pH to high pH values. Furthermore, they reported that UV radiation has a considerable effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs in both acidic and basic environments. Aggregation of CDs caused by carboxylic acid group deprotonation on the CDs surface can result in enhanced sensitivity of CDs at lower pH [56,144]. For example, Mingda, et al. Minda et al. reported that they evaluated as-prepared CD-AgNPs produced from Lycii fructus at pH values ranging from 2 to 10. The shift in absorbance at A525 nm/A400 nm in the presence of phoxim (30 μM) attracted a backdrop, reflecting the amount of CD-AgNP aggregation. The increased absorbance change revealed a higher degree of aggregation of the CDs-AgNPs. ΔA increased rapidly with pH from 2.0 to 6.0, most likely due to partial protonation of the functional groups on the surface of the CDs, resulting in incomplete CDs-AgNPs aggregation. However, when the pH ranged from 7.0 to 10.0, ΔA gradually reduced. It is possible that functional groups on the surface of CDs were deprotonated at alkaline conditions, resulting in a decrease in electrostatic force and therefore aggregation of CDs-AgNPs. As a result, pH = 6.0 was used throughout the experiments [62].

One of the pH response mechanisms is the protonation and deprotonation of the carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs [145], as schematically shown in Fig. 9(c). The mechanism of pH-sensitive PL was primarily focused on the deprotonation and protonation of acidic and basic groups in ground or excited states, which might alter the property and pace of transition processes, affecting the PL spectrum and intensity of the fluorophores [146,147]. CDs have a highly sophisticated structure, with graphite carbon or amorphous carbon serving as the main skeleton, and the surface is rich in carboxyl, hydroxyl, carbonyl, and other functional groups that can undergo protonation and deprotonation. As a result, the pH-dependent PL mechanism of CDs is perplexing and contentious. For example, Li et al. used hydrothermal treatment to create extremely luminous CDs utilizing tetrahydrofuran as a carbon source. The PL intensity fell linearly as the pH gained from 4 to 12. They hypothesized that the pH sensitivity of CDs was due to the reversible protonation and deprotonation of the CDs surface functional groups. However, no uniform conclusion has been reached about the specific structure responsible for CDs pH sensitivity [147].

However, Jia et al. hypothesized that protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs could cause electrostatic doping/charging and alter the Fermi level [148]. Tan et al. also believed that the deprotonation of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of C-dots at high pH leaded to the increase of surface negative charge, which caused the shift of Fermi level and restrained the electron transition of some emission centers by occupying the energy levels [149]. Zhao et al. hypothesized that the H-bond might produce significant vibration coupling of -OH at lower pH, causing the energy level to broaden and the conformational rigidity of C-dots to increase, resulting in a strong PL intensity. However, alkaline conditions could diminish the H-bonding effect during the deprotonation process, resulting in less vibrational coupling and more discrete energy levels, resulting in a decrease in CDs PL intensity [150]. The C-dots reported by Hu et al. displayed two emission regions at 550 and 630 nm, corresponding to the N- and P-states, respectively. The intensity of the peak at 550 nm declined dramatically as pH increased from 2 to 12, whilst the intensity of the peak at 630 nm increased continuously, resulting in a linear relationship between the PL intensity ratios of the two emission peaks (I550/I630) and pH. According to their claims, protonation of amine groups would discard unpaired electrons and lower electron density in CDs, resulting in a fall in PL intensity with a blue shift of the N-state PL peak. The pH fluctuation had little impact on the P-state's energy level; the peak at 630 nm remained steady across a large pH range of 2 to 12. The combination of P-related groups with OH− radicals at high pH would enhance the density of the P-state, promoting the emission at 630 nm [151].

Other studies state that reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of CDs might generally lead to pH dependency of CDs fluorescence. For example, Hu et al. discovered that the PL intensity of S-C- dots is pH-responsive. Adding NaOH can raise the pH from 3 to 9, resulting in an increase in PL intensity. The pH can then be changed from 9 to 3 using HCL to return to the initial value. Based on the findings, it is possible to conclude that the protonation and deprotonation of S-C-dots are related to the PL intensity [151]. Using the relationship between pH and zeta potential, Lian et al. demonstrated that the variation in fluorescence could be attributed to the reversible protonation and deprotonation of functional groups obtained from citric acid and dicyandiamide on the surface of GQDs [152]. The responses of both G-CDs and B-CDs to pH for a broad span of pH values were studied by Shi et al. They indicated that the strong pH-dependency of B-CDs would be due to its increased fluorescence intensity at 450 nm under 360 nm excitation when the pH is enhanced from 3.0 to 11.0. This result confirmed that B-CDs could be applied as fluorescent pH nano-sensor in the 3.5–10 pH range. This behavior of B-CDs can be related to the carboxyl group deprotonation, which results in the formation of a delocalized π bond and the enhancement of n electrons. The reversibility is very central for the pH probe [153]. Yang et al. found that increasing the pH from 1.48 to 7.56 increased the fluorescence strength of DMF-CDs linearly. The fluorescence peak can maintain its high intensity, while the trend of increasing fluorescence strength can slow down as the pH exceeds 8. The increase in PL intensity with rising pH could be attributed to carboxyl group deprotonation on the surface of the N-CDs. It may also cause the charging/electrostatic doping of the DMF-CDs by shifting the Fermi level. Furthermore, it demonstrates that deprotonation of the carboxyl group on the DMF-CDs at high pH levels might result in the formation of a novel surface state with a reduced bandgap. This behavior can also be attributed to DMF-CDs fluorescence emission at longer wavelengths [154]. Zhang et al. studied the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs throughout a pH range of 2–12. From pH = 2 to pH = 12, the fluorescence intensity decreased. The peak location remained nearly stable from pH = 2 to 6, and the peak location from pH 7 to 12 redshifted from 458 to 491 nm under stimulation at 350 nm. The functional groups on the surface of GQDs may have been protonated or deprotonated [142].

Another mechanism proposed for CD pH dependence is the transfer of a proton from the protonated nitrogen structure to the conjugated carbon structure (Fig. 9(d)). Fluorescence intensity exhibits an inverse relationship with pH in the range of 5.0 to 13.5, according to Qian et al. This demonstrates the significance of proton present in NCQD fluorescence amplification. They stated that XPS measurements revealed two forms of nitrogen atoms, graphitic and pyridinic nitrogen, and that only the latter can accept protons. By gradually decreasing the pH, pyridinic nitrogen atoms can be protonated, and this proton transfer to the conjugated carbon structure may result in CD fluorescence enhancement [141].

Other than the energy level shift produced by H+/OH, protective cell is also one of the mechanisms of pH-sensitive PL in CDs. pH-sensitive CDs with various surface modifications were described by Kong et al. (2014). According to their hypothesis, absorbed OH could passivate the surface defects of C-dots and form a surface modified protective shell layer, which isolated C-dots and lowered the nonradiative recombination rate, leading to an increase in PL intensity and blue-shifting of the emission peak, whereas H+ could destroy the protective shell passivated by OH, create new surface defects on C-dots, and enlarge the size of CDs by prolonging the length of conjugated chain, resulting in PL quenching and red-shifting of emission peak [155]. Furthermore, Guo et al. (2018) and Liu et al. (2017) ascribed the pH-dependent PL to the protective shell generated by absorbed OH passivation on the surface detects of CDs [156,157]. The schematic of the protective shell mechanism as a pH response mechanism for CDs is presented in Fig. 9(e). Zheng et al. measured the FL emission spectra of CDs at various pH levels. For their experiment, the excitation wavelength was 330 nm. According to Zheng et al., the FL intensity of CDs-OH was unaffected by pH ranging from 5.0 to 11.0. However, the FL intensity of CDs-H increases with pH below 5.0. Actually, increasing the pH caused carboxylic deprotonation on the surface of the CDs-H and the formation of a negative charge “protective shell” with a decreased non-radiative recombination rate. However, above pH 5.0, a homogeneous “protective shell” was formed on the surface of CDs-OH by deprotonation of carboxylic groups [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs natural product for pesticides detection
ce:section.ce:para: As described in the previous section, extensive use of pesticides to escalate the agricultural yield has posed increasing health risks to humans, even when used at lower concentrations. In recent years, natural product CDs are an emerging alternative to semiconductor sensors and some researchers have widely used them, one of which is pesticide detection. Wang et al.(2018) used egg white as a carbon precursor in the synthesis of CDs, then its fluorescence was quenched by Au(III) and restored by thiocholine (TCh) produced by acetylthiocholine (ATCh) hydrolyzed by AChE for sensitive organophosphate detection with detection limit up to 4.47 μM [47]. Zheng et al. (2018) using green synthesis of CDs with modified Lycii Fructus precursors with AgNPs as colorimetric sensors for phoxim detection with limit of detection reached 0.04 μM [62]. In addition to CDs modified with metals, co-doping variations of some heteroatoms also show very interesting prospects in nanoprobes. Shi et al. [158] has acquired excellent FL properties and low toxicity from N-doping, S-CDs due to the close atomic radius of the atom N to C and the similar electronegativity of the S to C atoms. N, P-CDs also display excellent for electrochemical properties and high fluorescence QY [159]. In addition, Li et al. (2020) performed the synthesis of green CDs with three different fluorescence emission spectrums by changing the doping mode. The comparison carried out, namely the synthesis of CDs doped N and flavonoids (N-CDs-Fla) obtained QY results of 26.2% for the detection of Fenitrothion (Fen) with a detection limit of 0.36 nM, then N, S, B, CDs-Fla with QY 33.8% for the detection of Dinoseb (Din) which reached 0.66 nM, and N, S-CDs-Fla showed the highest QY of 46.6% for Dithianon (Dit) with a detection limit of 0.28 nM [41]. N-based sensors, S-CDs-Fla with the detection of three types of pesticides, prove that there is potential in polyatomic co-doping CDs in pesticide sensing.

In addition, Ghosh et al. prepared CDs that mainly included C-, O- and N-containing surface functional groups from the Tagetes erecta flower commonly called as Marigold flower via hydrothermal technique for selective detection of chlorpyrifos and quinalphos pesticides [40]. The as-synthesized CDs possessed a negative surface charge (−14.91 mV) which changed to positive with the addition of the pesticides due to the aggregation effect. The sensing application was investigated by dispersing the CDs in different pesticides, but the CDs displayed marked specific responses either as turn-off fluorescence in the case of chlorpyrifos or fluorescence turn-on in the presence of quinalphos. The practical utility was confirmed through the rapid detection of the two pesticides in rice and fruit samples with good accuracy. Furthermore, the use of luminescence power in CDs as selective and sensitive sensors in testing and quenching mechanisms of CDs-pesticides. Leaves are recognized as the most excellent carbon precursors by reason of exhibiting higher quantum yields than other parts of the plant like seeds and stem. Further, the same team reported, Ghosh et al. the facile synthesis of CDs from the leaves of Calotropis procera by hydrothermal method for effective detection of isoprothiolane. CDs provided an excellent quantum yield (71.95%) without any toxic agents or surface passivation chemicals this result is better than other product natural CDs reported in this review. It is noteworthy that an excellent quantum yield resulted from the functional groups (OH, NH, CO, and CN bond) derived from the carbon precursors themselves. Furthermore, the synthesized CDs have a strong peak around 416 nm in the UV–Visible spectra of CDs, implying that it was spawned by the n → π* transition of CO bonds over the surface of CDs. The emission peak of CDs was significantly decreased with the addition of isoprothiolane, with the detection limit found to be 11.58 nM (3.36 ppb), and the fluorescence intensity of the probe was not hampered vastly under different pH conditions [42]. The nanoprobe was further successfully applied in rice and fruit samples effectively.

Shekarbeygi et al. synthesized CDs from aqueous and alcoholic blue, yellow, and red rose flower extract through a hydrothermal approach. They also studied the outcome of different synthesis methods on the optical properties of the prepared CDs. The CDs obtained from the alcoholic extract of yellow petals were more stable and had a high quantum yield of 45.67%. The reported CDs were efficiently employed for the detection of diazinon with a limit of detection of 0.01 μM [39]. Furthermore, the Tafreshi et al. reported cauliflower-derived CDs through a hydrothermal approach for application in food safety and environmental monitoring. The CDs could detect diazinon, glyphosate, and amicarbazone pesticides through turn-off fluorescence without super dialen and bromacil interference. These carbonaceous nanoprobes demonstrated good fluorescence-based sensing behavior for diazinon, glyphosate, and amicarbazone in real fruit samples, though they failed to distinguish the three pesticides in their mixture [37].

Alvandi, et al. (2021) developed an investigation related to multicolored CDs fluorescence discoloration that depends on their concentration with carbon sources derived from Greengage Plums to detect pesticides (bromoxynil, diazinon, glyphosate, malathion, and deltamethrin). The maximum fluorescence intensity of CDs is yellow (1.2 mg/mL), green (0.6 mg/mL), and blue (0.12 mg/mL) at excitation wavelengths of 460 nm, 430 nm, and 360 nm, respectively, resulting in maximum emissions of approximately 510, 520, and 450 nm, respectively. An excitation wavelength of 460 nm indicates the energy level of N-related defect states between n surface bands. Then at a wavelength of 430 nm, it shows optimal excitation, which corresponds to the transition of the n-π* gap and the wavelength of 360 nm with the π -π* representing the transition of the core state on the CDs. Blue discoloration occurs as the concentration of CDs decreases. In addition, functional groups with N- and O- have a key role in various emission intensities. Nitrogen atoms on the surface, when confirmed by FTIR, can reduce the energy gap from the energy state with nitrogen bonds so that they are visible in yellow emissions. This nitrogen-bound energy state is below LUMO at the core and edge states, respectively reflecting the π -π* transition (blue emission) and the n-π* transition (green emission) can be seen in Fig. 10 [160]. Disturbances in the energy transition from the core state LUMO to the N bond state on CDs lead to electron recombination for most nitrogen bonds. This leads to yellow emissions. Inspired by the role of the material, natural CDs of products derived from Greengage Plums synthesized by hydrothermal method managed to achieve 48% fluorescence QY at 360 nm excitation [48]. The summary of the CDs obtained from natural product for the detection of various pesticides molecules is provided in Table 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application aptasensor-based carbon dots for detection of residue pesticides
ce:section.ce:para: According to the history of pesticide use, pesticides have generated severe problems in food safety and environmental contamination. Many researchers and scientists are working hard to create fast, sensitive, and highly specific pesticide detection methods in order to properly manage pesticide contamination in human protection. In addition to employing biomass materials / natural products to synthesize CDs for pesticide detection, researchers created aptasensors by combining CDs-based biosensors with aptamers.

The aptamer is a short-stranded oligonucleotide (ssRNA or ssDNA) that was created using the Systematic Evolution of Ligands by Exponential Enrichment (SELEX) approach, which was initially reported by Ellington and Szostak [161], and subsequently independently by Tuerk and Gold [162] in 1990. The aptamer has the best specificity with high affinity and sensitivity for binding to its target molecules such as nucleic acids, proteins, metal ions, and other tiny molecules [161,163]. Furthermore, compared to antibodies with great stability and modest dimensions, aptamers are also straightforward to synthesize in vitro, quick to modify, and adaptable in design sequences. As a result of their contribution that can also increase detection with efficient and outstanding binding constants with dissociation constants (Kd) in the micromolar to picomolar range [164,165] aptamers can be used in the field of sensors as a novel recognition element. Due to these advantages, the renewal of aptamers has attracted the interest of researchers, which are employing them to construct sensors to detect pesticide residues [53], pathogenic bacteria E.coli [166], heavy metal ions [167], and other harmful substances [168,169].

In this study, we will focus on CDs-based aptasensor applications for pesticide residue detection in order to inspire other researchers to identify and develop novel aptasensor applications. The scheme below depicts the use of CDs and aptamers in optical sensors to detect the presence of pesticide residues. (Fig. 11 ).

The selection of the appropriate pesticide-specific aptamer is the most important factor in the construction of highly effective aptasensors for pesticide detection. Therefore, there are numerous research focusing on the selection of pesticide-specific aptamers. For example, Bala et al. (2018) synthesized nanoprobes based on CdTe@CdS QDs using polymers containing guanidinium, specifically homopolymer poly(N-(3-guanidinopropyl) methacrylamide) (PGPMA) as quencher and aptamer as recognition elements for highly sensitive malathion detection with a detection limit of 4 pM with a linear range from 0.01 nm to 1 μM. In this detection platform (Fig. 12 ), observations were made of a change that occurred in the fluorescence emission of QDs after the addition of malathion. The interaction of the aptamer with the malathion will turn off its fluorescence signal, causing the QDs to be quenched. Despite the absence of malathion in the sample, the polymer will still interact with the aptamer through electrostatic interaction and thus will restore the fluorescence of the unaffected QDs [64]. Futhermore, Saberi, et al. (2019) developed label-free acetamiprid-specific DNA aptamers (ACPs) introduced with cationic CDs (cCDs) for the construction of fluorescent aptasensors as acetamiprid residue detection. Aptasensors based on cCDs have been proven to be sensitive and selective, with a detection limit of 0.3 nM and a linear range of 1.6 to 120 nM [66].

Furthermore, in addition to the fluorescence method, Liu et al. (2020) attempted to develop aptasensors with the principle of ECL luminescence performance using a spherical MoS2/CdS nanosphere with good size and morphology and ECL luminescence performance. Nanosphere MoS2/CdS is thought to have a larger surface area and higher electron density, resulting in a stronger catalytic effect on K2S2O88 and a higher luminescence intensity. At the same time, the Ag/CQDs complex is produced as receptors and utilized to label aptamers. It can have a very large absorption band between 300 and 600 nm due to the synergistic effects of AgNPs and CQDs. The absorption bands and emission spectra of MoS2/CdS will overlap, allowing high Ag/CQDs quantum findings to accomplish efficient energy resonance transmission. The “signal on” aptasensors were then developed using a very efficient ECL-RET approach between the MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQD nanospheres. The complementary chain (cDNA) of chlorpyrifos aptamer was modified with AuNPs@MoS2/CdS/GCE and subsequently coupled with Ag/CQDs-aptamer using the base-complementary pairs approach. Efficient energy resonance transfer between MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQDs lowers ECL emissions. Chlorpyrifos can destroy the double helical structure, leading Ag/CQDs-aptamers to lag off the electrode surface. The ECL intensity of MoS2/CdS is also recovered (signal-on). The scheme of the ECL aptasensor fabrication process is shown in Fig. 13 . This ECL aptasensor based on the energy resonance transfer of MoS2/CdS and Ag/CQD nanospheres can detect very sensitive chlorpyrifos, with a detection limit of 0.35 fM (S / N = 3) and a linear correlation of 10 nM - 1.0 fM [68].

Finally, reviews that aim to evaluate advanced CDs-based aptasensors with their sensing performance, such as the aptasensor principle, detection limits, linear correlation coefficients, and aptamer sequences employed for pesticide specificity, can be displayed and summarized in Table 3 .

Persistent concerns about the impact of pesticide residues on human life and the environment have given rise to a long-term effort to develop novel sensors. There have been several great analyses of the sensor techniques available for pesticide analysis over the last decade [2,170], but carbon nanomaterial fluorescence-based systems still have many chances to be investigated further. This is proven by the large number of researchers who are developing fluorescence-based optical sensors for pesticide detection, such as the production of carbon dots (CDs) or carbon nanodots (CNDs) using diverse synthesis and modification method [171–173]. Carbon dots (CDs) have been shown to be good candidates for optical sensors due to their adjustable optical properties, large surface area specifics, high free energy surfaces, and outstanding biocompatibility. These carbon nanomaterials are ideal for the development of pesticide detection sensing techniques. In addition to its distinctive optical features, the fundamental factors of the carbon source of nanomaterials play a significant role in the development of carbon dots (CDs). CDs can be synthesized from both chemical and natural carbon sources. In this review, we highlight carbon sources manufactured as CDs from natural products for use in pesticide detection applications, as well as modification CDs with aptamer recognition elements utilized in the same application as aptasensors. Natural product CDs derivatives have several advantages: First, organic precursors are derived from a range of sustainable biomass carbon sources, are inexpensive, simple, and abundant to create, have high yields, and are biocompatible. Second, organic carbon sources naturally contain heteroatoms, and heteroatomic doped CDs exhibit outstanding photoluminescence [174]. Finally, the synthetic method utilized to create CND is easy, quick, and, to some extent, environmentally beneficial. As a result, organic products are likely a promising source of starting carbon in green and efficient CND synthesis. Many studies are also focusing on the development of new natural biological waste, innovative techniques prepared and better materials, and cost-effective pesticide detecting equipment. Besides the benefits of natural product-based CDs in pesticide detection applications, CDs were also developed with an introduction to aptasensors.

Many researchers throughout the world have developed CDs-based aptasensors. The limit detection of aptasensors is highly good and can detect pesticides very sensitively. CDs paired with these aptamers are the most promising, and their performance can be improved further by selecting pesticide-specific aptamers, which can boost sensitivity and lower detection limits. Furthermore, aptamer-based CDs exhibit good specificity and a high affinity for pesticide filtration. As a result, future aptamer research should concentrate on screening more possible aptamers for other types of pesticides with high specificity and affinity. The use of nanotechnology in biosensors such as natural products CDs and aptamer-based CDs has the potential to usher in the future in biosensor technology. Nanomaterial-based biosensors are still in the early stages of development and are often only used in clinical diagnosis, food sample analysis, and environmental monitoring in a limited capacity. Newer sorts of development in the field of nanotechnology are required so that collaborative biosensor techniques and nanotechnologies can be more productive. The application of biosensor technology in the environmental field to date has not been fully explored compared to medical applications. Most commercialized biosensors are related to medical field applications, while only a few are adapted for environmental sample monitoring. Therefore, tremendous efforts are still required to create better and more reliable devices that enable pesticide detection to the greatest degree possible with maintaining high sensitivity and selectivity. Improving the stability of biocomponent storage is also important. This is a fascinating study into the enormous potential of natural product CDs and the incorporation of aptamers into CDs a major challenge. We really believe that this study can spark novel perspectives for researchers to create better natural product-based CDs sensors for pesticide detection applications, as well as CDs with the introduction of more diversified pesticide-specific aptamers.

This research was funded by the Padjadjaran University, Indonesia, through research grant scheme of Padjadjaran Scholarship for Graduate Program (BUPP), contract number 1549/UN6.3.1/PT/2023 dated on March 27, 2023.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yan, H. Li, and X. Su, “Review of optical sensors for pesticides,” TrAC - Trends Anal. Chem., vol. 103, pp. 1–20, 2018, doi: 10.1016/j.trac.2018.03.004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent approaches to improving selectivity and sensitivity of enzyme-based biosensors for organophosphorus pesticides: a review
